author: @StephanLuik1
text: I used this method once in awhile. There's a special nozzle that you can attach to the paint stripper, so you can direct the heat better. When removing integrated circuits, beware of ESD. Use a wrist strap. Also, tweezers are a bit more gentle than a screw driver. Using flux will ease the process. With power supplies, make sure the capacitors are drained before touching them.

author: @townbell2248
text: Could you make a video on repurposing components from circuit boards

author: @richardsandwell2285
text:
What a great idea using a heat gun, I have spent too long with a soldering iron and solder sucker. 
This will save me time.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Aha .... I happen to know what it smells like if you do that. Your neighbours know too now I guess. Probably the whole neighbourhood.
Actually it's common to remove smd silicon with a small heat gun. Usually these can be reused after that. If you stick a pin under it, slightly pull and heat with hot air it will not be hotter than when it was soldered by the reflow process.
For through hole components it's better to take them off wire by wire if the component shape allows it. Just with a solder iron. If you have a high pin count like a DIL package you can cut from above and pull the wires out one by one. The component will be lost but usually that's not an issue. Tweezer solder irons exist and can help in many situations.  It can be difficult to remove a through hole component due to pcb design. If a wire is placed in a pad which has high thermal conductivity to a large copper plane or maybe 4, 6 or 8 internal copper planes it can be very hard to remove it. Preheaters exist for this kind of situation. When preheated you only need to add the additional heat to get to the melting point from the preheated situation. Not from room temperature.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: When you're removing toroidals, if they are for rewinding you can just snip the base of the copper wire. Heat is only needed when they will be reused in the same form.

author: @garethlaw4884
text: Good work Luke 👍

author: @anneinfurna8528
text:
The technique that i was taught is to use a standard soldering Gun, and a tool called a "Soldapullit". What that does is as you isolate the component leg, you warm it up with the soldering gun, and apply the rubber tip of the SoldaPullit to the molten solder, it triggers with a 
finger and basically 'Sucks the molten Solder off the component leg and you do the other ones there and the component drops right off with no damage. What makes it neat is you can remelt the solder and use it as it gets ejected out the front of the tip when you reset the tool.
Neat Gizmo -- Look them up!

replies.text: Indeed those small (literally) suckers are handy for lot of stuff but not really suitable for small pads.

author: @brendanjohn1014
text: Generally I use a soldering iron and a desolder pump. Liquify the solder then suck it away... tales two ticks and you do not damage the component.

author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
Remember that if you use parchment paper and a marker so that you have some heat protection for the board so you dont scorch anything that you don't want heat damaged; parchment paper can push through the pins and heat the solder without "burning" the board for later purposes.

Keep it up mate, you're doing fine!
